user_prompt: Explain Bernoulli’s principle using an airplane wing in outside flight, visualizing airflow speed and pressure differences; narration explains cause and lift effect; no humans on screen.

style_bible.visual_style: Realistic 3D rendering with smooth airflow visualization using translucent colored streams; clean and modern aesthetic emphasizing clarity of physical phenomena.
style_bible.camera_rules: Camera remains outside the airplane wing, avoiding any interior or human presence; smooth continuous movements with no jump cuts; framing varies from medium to close-up to highlight airflow and pressure effects.
style_bible.lighting_rules: Soft natural daylight with slight directional sunlight from above-left to create gentle shadows and depth; cool color temperature to emphasize scientific clarity.
style_bible.continuity_rules: Primary subject and environment remain consistent across all scenes; airflow visualization style and color coding remain unchanged; camera movements maintain screen direction continuity.

scenes.prompt:
SCENE INTENT: Show an airplane wing in flight with visible airflow patterns to introduce Bernoulli’s principle.

GLOBAL CONTINUITY:
- primary subject: airplane wing in steady flight
- environment: clear blue sky with distant clouds
- explanatory state:
  - Scene 1: baseline condition (observation)

SHOT 1 (0–3s):
- framing: medium shot, wing centered slightly left
- lens: 35mm
- camera movement: slow dolly forward
- subject & action: airflow streams appear smoothly flowing over and under the wing by 1s, steady by 3s
- environment details: blue sky background, soft white clouds far away, subtle wing reflections
- lighting: soft sunlight from above-left, natural shadows
- cinematic look: crisp focus, moderate contrast, slight depth of field on wing edges

SHOT 2 (3–7s):
- framing: closer medium shot focusing on wing cross-section
- lens: 50mm
- camera movement: slow pan left to right following airflow
- subject & action: airflow speed lines visible, uniform thickness and spacing over and under wing
- environment details: sky remains consistent, wing texture visible
- lighting: consistent with shot 1
- cinematic look: enhanced clarity on airflow streams, smooth gradients

SHOT 3 (7–10s):
- framing: close-up on wing leading edge and airflow split
- lens: 85mm
- camera movement: static
- subject & action: airflow divides above and below wing, steady flow patterns
- environment details: minimal sky background
- lighting: soft directional light highlighting wing curvature
- cinematic look: shallow depth of field on wing surface

AVOID: text, logos, subtitles, watermarks, extra limbs, distorted faces, flicker, glitch, people, human, face, hands, cockpit
scenes.negative_prompt: text, logos, subtitles, watermarks, extra limbs, distorted faces, flicker, glitch, people, human, face, hands, cockpit
scenes.camera: Slow dolly forward to medium wing shot, then slow pan left to right focusing on airflow, ending with static close-up on leading edge
scenes.audio_cue: Here we see an airplane wing in steady flight, with air flowing smoothly over and under its curved surface. Notice how the airflow splits at the wing's leading edge, setting the stage for the forces that keep the plane aloft.

scenes.prompt:
SCENE INTENT: Reveal the cause of lift by visualizing airflow speed differences and pressure zones around the wing.

GLOBAL CONTINUITY:
- primary subject: airplane wing in steady flight
- environment: clear blue sky with distant clouds
- explanatory state:
  - Scene 2: mechanism revealed (cause)

SHOT 1 (0–3s):
- framing: medium close-up on wing upper and lower surfaces
- lens: 50mm
- camera movement: slow tilt downward following airflow
- subject & action: airflow lines above wing accelerate and become thinner and brighter by 2s; below wing lines remain thicker and slower
- environment details: sky background, subtle wing texture
- lighting: directional sunlight from above-left
- cinematic look: enhanced contrast between fast and slow airflow

SHOT 2 (3–7s):
- framing: close-up on wing cross-section
- lens: 85mm
- camera movement: slow pan right to left
- subject & action: animated pressure zones appear as color-coded translucent overlays—blue low pressure above wing, red higher pressure below
- environment details: minimal sky background
- lighting: consistent with shot 1
- cinematic look: clear visualization of pressure differences with smooth gradients

SHOT 3 (7–10s):
- framing: medium shot showing wing and airflow
- lens: 35mm
- camera movement: slow dolly backward
- subject & action: arrows appear indicating force direction upward on wing surface
- environment details: sky and clouds consistent
- lighting: natural sunlight
- cinematic look: balanced contrast, clear force visualization

AVOID: text, logos, subtitles, watermarks, extra limbs, distorted faces, flicker, glitch, people, human, face, hands, cockpit
scenes.negative_prompt: text, logos, subtitles, watermarks, extra limbs, distorted faces, flicker, glitch, people, human, face, hands, cockpit
scenes.camera: Slow tilt downward to medium close-up, then slow pan right to left on wing cross-section, ending with slow dolly backward to medium wing shot
scenes.audio_cue: The air moves faster over the curved upper surface, creating lower pressure, while slower air beneath maintains higher pressure. This pressure difference generates an upward force, known as lift, acting on the wing.

scenes.prompt:
SCENE INTENT: Show the effect of lift on the airplane wing and conclude the explanation.

GLOBAL CONTINUITY:
- primary subject: airplane wing in steady flight
- environment: clear blue sky with distant clouds
- explanatory state:
  - Scene 3: outcome explained (effect)

SHOT 1 (0–3s):
- framing: wide shot of entire airplane wing angled slightly upward
- lens: 35mm
- camera movement: slow crane upward
- subject & action: wing visibly rises with animated upward arrows representing lift force
- environment details: sky with soft clouds, sunlight reflections on wing
- lighting: soft directional sunlight
- cinematic look: vibrant colors, clear depiction of motion

SHOT 2 (3–7s):
- framing: medium shot focusing on wing root and airflow
- lens: 50mm
- camera movement: slow pan left to right
- subject & action: airflow streams maintain speed difference, pressure zones remain visible
- environment details: sky background
- lighting: consistent natural light
- cinematic look: smooth gradients, clear force depiction

SHOT 3 (7–10s):
- framing: close-up on wingtip with trailing airflow
- lens: 85mm
- camera movement: static
- subject & action: vortices form at wingtip illustrating induced drag
- environment details: minimal sky
- lighting: soft highlight on wingtip
- cinematic look: sharp focus on vortices

AVOID: text, logos, subtitles, watermarks, extra limbs, distorted faces, flicker, glitch, people, human, face, hands, cockpit
scenes.negative_prompt: text, logos, subtitles, watermarks, extra limbs, distorted faces, flicker, glitch, people, human, face, hands, cockpit
scenes.camera: Slow crane upward wide shot, then slow pan left to right medium wing root shot, ending with static close-up on wingtip vortices
scenes.audio_cue: This pressure difference creates lift, allowing the wing to rise and support the airplane in flight. At the wingtip, vortices form, illustrating the forces that also contribute to drag, balancing the flight dynamics.
